## A Sociological Test

Moving from right to left or from righr bank to left bank, we start with L'Aurore: 'Cheeky Françoise Dorin is going to be in hot warer with our ofee-nosed, Marxist intelligentsia (the two things go together). The auchor of Un sale egoisle shows no respecr for the solemn boredom, profound emptiness and verciginous nullity which characterize so many so-called "a:vantgarde' chearrical productions. She dares ro profane wich sacrile gious laughter the notorious"incommunicabiliry" which is the alpha: and omega of the contemporary srage. And this perverse reactionary, who Hatrers the lowest appetires of consumer society, far from acknowledging the crror of her ways and wearing her boulevard playwright's repuration with humility, has the impudence to prefer the jolliry of Sacha Guitry, or Feydeau's bedroom farces, to the darkness visible of Margucrite Duras or Arrabal. This is a crime for which she will not casily be for given. Especially since she commits it wich cheerfulness and gaiety, using all the dreadful devices which make lasring successes' (Gilberr Guilleminaud. L' Aurore, 12 Januaty 1973).

remaric use of formulations that are ambiguous even as regards the oppositions involved: "Ir ought to be a runawaly success', "asly cunning, a perfect masrery of che crichs of the trade', 'Philippe has the plum rôle', all formulae which could equally be taken pejoratively. And we even fnd, surfacing through irs denial, a hint of the orher ruch (\*Only morose pedants will probe roo far .. .') or even of theplaintruth, bur doubly neurralized byambiguicy and denial ('and nor just because ir is a class truth').

Le Monde offers a perfecr example of ostentariously neurral discourse, even-handedly dismissing borh sides, both the overtly political discourse of L'Axrore and che disdainful silence of Le Nouvel Observateur: The simple, or simplistic, argument is complicared by a very subele "wotier"" srructure, as if chere were rwo plays overlapping. Onc by Françoise Dorin, a convenrional aurhor, the other invenred by Philippe Roussel, who tries to rake "the turning" ro wards modem thearre. This conceir performs a circular movemenr, like a boomerang. Françoise Dorin deliberarely exposes the Boulevard clichés which Philippe atracks and, through his voice, delivers a violent denunciarion of the bourgeoisie. On the second Aoor, she contrasts this language wich thar of a young author whom she assails with equal vigour. Finally, the trajectory brings the weapon back onto the Boulevard srage, and che fuciliries of che mechanism are unmasked by the devices of the traditional theatre, which are shown to have losr norhing of their value. Philippe can declare himself a "couragcously light" playwright, invencing "characrers who talk like real people"; he can claim that his arr is "withour frontiers" and therefore non-political. However, the

demonstration is entirely distorted by the model avant-garde author chosen by Françoise Dorin. Vankovicz is an epigone of Marguerice Duras, a vaguely militant, belated existentialist. He is parodic in the extreme, like rhe theatre that is denounced here ("A black curtain and a scaffold certainly help!" or the rirle of the play: "Do take a litcle angst in your coffee, Mr. Karsov"). The audience sniggers at this derisive picture of modern drama; rhe denunciation of the bour geoisie is an amusing provocation inasmuch as it rebounds onto an odious victim and finishes him off. . . To the extent that it reflects the state of bourgeois thearre and reveals its systems of defence, Le Tournant can be regarded as an imporiant wvork. Few plays let slip so much anxiety abour an "external" threat and recuperate it with so much unconscious fury' (L. uis Dandrel, Le Monde, 13 January 1973).

The ambiguity which Robert Kanters was already beginning to cultivate here reaches its peak. The argument is 'simple or simplistic', rake your pick; the play is split in rwo, offering two works fot the readet's choice, a 'violent' but 'recuperatory' critique of the bourgeoisie and a defence of non-policical art. For anyone naive enough to ask whether the critic is 'for of against', whether he finds the play \*good or bad', there are two answers: fist, the observation by an 'objecrive informant' wich a duty towards truth that the avant-garde author portrayed is 'parodic in the extreme' and that 'the audience sniggers' (but wichout our knowing where the cricic stands in relation to this audience, and therefore whar the sniggering signifies), and then, after a series of judgements that are held y on wor ut ed   uell l Po.

'Françoise Dorin really knows a thing or swo. She's a hrst-rate recuperator and terribly well-bred. Her Tournant is an excellent Boulevard comedy, which runs mainly on bad faith and demagogy The lady wants to prove that avant-garde cheatre is a dog's dinner. To do so, she takes a big bag of tricks and, needless to say, as soon as she pulls one out the au dience rolls in the aisles and calls for more. Our author, who was just waiting far that, does it again. She gives us a young trendy leftist playwright called Vankowicz—get it?-—and puts him in various ridiculous, uncomfortable and rather shady siruations, to show that this young gentleman is no more disinterested, no less bourgeois, than you and I What common sense, Mme. Dorin, what lucidity, what honeitly! You at least have the courage to stand by your opinions, and very healrhy, redwhite-and-blue ones they are too\* (Philippe Tesson, Le Canard Enchaine, 17 March 1973 [italics in all foregoing <quotarions are mine]).

Those who are held to be distinguished have the privilege of not worrying about their distinction; they can leave it to the objective mechanisms which provide theit distinctive properties and to the 'sense of distinction' which sreers them away from everything 'common' Where the petir bourgeois or nouveau riche 'overdoes it', betraying his own insecurity, bourgeois discrerion signals its presence by a sort of ostentatious discretion, sobriety and understatement, a refusal of everything which is 'showy', 'fashy' and pretencious, and which devalues iselfby the very intention of distinction.

lief in the value of the game which makes the game and endlessly chenicd e itation'tr'cultue  popularization' wch stake, conceals a collusion that is no less indispensable to the production and reproduction of the illusio, the fundamental recognition of the culcural game and its stakes Distinction and pretension, high culture and middle-brow culure—like, elsewhere, high fashion and fashion, haute coiffure and coiffure, and so on-only exist through each other, and it is the relation, or rarher, the objective collaboration of their respective pro duction apparatuses and clients which produces the value of culcure and the need ro possess it. It is in these struggles between objectively com plicir opponents that the value of culture is genetated, or, which amounts to the same thing, belief in the value of culcure, interesr in culture and the interest of culture—which are not self-evident, although one of the effects of the game is to induce belief in the innareness of the de sire to play and the pleasure of playing. It is barbarism to ask what culture is for; to allow the hypothesis that culture might be devoid of intrinsic interest, and that interest in culture is not a natural properry— unequally disttibuted, as if to separate rhe barbarians from the elect—but a simple social artifact, a particular form of fetishism; to raise the question of the interest of activities which are called disinterested because e so to introduce the question of the interest of disinterestedness.

The struggle itself thus produces effects which tend to disguise the vy exic o t tgg I te elatnho the ifnt cass with culture can be described indifferently either in the language farepouc the aps which e the essence o he race It is o accient h—part o roon, whosip y his pti-burg or — cul wh i cualgame  strogly e heomina clas  h okemen imbued wirh a sense of theit cultural unworchiness.

The farter merely contest one culture in the name of another, counteiposing a culture dominated within the relatively auronomous field of cultural production and distribution (which does not make it the culture of the dominated) to a dominant cultute; in so doing they fulfil the traditional role of a cultural avant-garde which, by its very existence, helps to keep the cultural game functioning

The dominated classes intervene in the symbolic struggles to appropriare the distinctive properties which give the distinctive life-styles their physiognomy and especially in the struggles to define the legitimate properties and the legitimate mode of appropriation, only as a passive ref erence point, a foil. The nature against which culture is here constructed is nothing other than what is 'populat', 'low', 'vulgar', 'common' This means that anyone who wanrs to 'succeed in life' must pay for his accession to everything which defines truly humane humans by a change of nature, a 'social promotion' experienced as an ontological promotion, a process of 'civilization' (Hugo speaks somewhere of the 'civilizing power of Art'), a leap from nature to culture, from the animal to the human; but having internalized che class struggle, which is at the very heart of culcute, he is condemned to shame, horror, even harred of the old Adam, his language, his body and his tastes, and of everyching he was bound to, which he is now separared by a frontier more absolure rhan any taboo.

## The Modes of Appropriation of the Work of Art

![](../../Distinction A Social Critique of the Judgement of Taste (Pierre Bourdieu, Richard Nice)/images/29a7ed8d217d9d29d61dea36359e975281881e901c567105c048516e19e9d86c.jpg)

## A Cosy Samovar-StyleBedroom

Isabelle d'Omano, the Minister's sisser-inlaw, has made ber bedroom the centre-piece of her a pariment. A baroque master piece.

'I know how I want to live. Decorarion is a way of expressing ic.' Scorning fashion and irs conventions, she has applied this principle throughour her apartment--a rhapsody of colours, imiration green marble and Venetian blinds—and especially in her bedroom An a)- most timeless and yer very up-todate room, which also serves as Isabelle's srudy when she is working (markering the 'Sisley' range of cosmetics her husband launched three years ago), as a TV room for her five children and sometimes, since it communicares with the reception rooms. as a second salon for big diner partis. nally dull library, sumpruous and boring: she has turned it inro something warm and 'cosy', as she puts ir.

First, by having a circular balcony built around the walls almost halfway up. . . . By organizing movement in the room around a centrepiece: the bed. And quire a bed it is! . . . Isabelle d'Omano likes 'mus cular' furniture and wanced 'a bed which suggests a gondola.' Her up. holsterer had his work cur our for a year and a half!

By flouting all the classical rules and combining different styles of furnirure—in facr, every style A Louis XVI inla id roll-top desk. upholstered 'ub' easy-chairs and Sec ond Empire fireside chairs, an enormous eighreenth cenrury crystal chandelier from the La Granja works, boughe from a Madrid anrique-dealer, one or cvvo lirtle laceninereenth-cenrury English stands holding plants, books and anorcid the ony foer hat lases', glass lamps bought for a song at Drouor, wich modern shades, rwo e b from a cabinet-maker. By mingling colours and fabrics wich a cerrain audacity. . By sprinkling the ensemble, nor with knick-knacks ("they're pointless') bur with dozens of photos ... Wich wicker baskets full of bric-à- brac. With children's mugs bristling with pencils. Wich novels, exhibition catalogues, magazines on interior decorarion (she cuts out the

useful addresses and sticks them in scrapbooks), scaccered all over the place. And ocher very personal details, such as the painted faience ciles surrounding the chimney-piece .. . In shorr, byadoptingan original, peisonal styleof decoration.So much so that designer Henri Samuel, who acted as technical adviser, replied, when I asked him to define this bedroom: 'It's pure d'Ornano, and thar's a complimenr!

D. de Saint-Sauveur. Le Figaro Magazine (Madame Figaro), 7 October, 1978.

One's immediate intuition should be followed—for the purpose of resting it-when it sees in the reachers' taste for the austerity of pure works, Bach or Braque, Breche or Mondrian, che same asceric disposition that is expressed in all cheir pracrices, and when it senses in chese seemingly innocent choices the symptom of a similar, but merely bereerhidden, relacionship to sexuality or money; or when it divines the whole view of the world and of exisrence that is expressed in the taste for the delights of boulevard theate or the Impressionists, for Renoir's rosy women, Boudin's sunlic beaches or Dufy's stage sers.

As is clearly seen in theatre or paincing (bur the same is true of the oer ars), what emerge chrough the discontinuous or disparate indices which have to be used to take measurements are (wo antagonistic relations to the work of art or, more precisely, cwo modes of aesthetic appropriation expressing (wo opposite asser structures. Thus, how is one to explain why the median price paid for a theatre cickec rises from 4.17 francs among tcachers (less than is paid by junior execurives in the privace sector, 4.61, and the public sector, 4.77) to 6.09 for public-sector senior executives, 7.00 for the professions, 7.58 for private-secror iv which gives one theordinary hierarchy of the fracrions distribured by volume of economic capital?And how does one explain why the hierarchy of the fractions is inverted if their rate of represcntation inthe way of appropriating it.

mission charge of a museum as being cheap or very cheap riss vey latvela os w  oesio bisiuish  odal distri (reasonable—very cheap).

## A Grand Bour geois Unique among His Kind'\*

5., a lawyer aged 45, is the son of a lawyer and his family belongs to the Parisian grande bourgeoisie. ## 'Heirlooms? Don't make me laugh'

## A 'Truly Classical' University Teacher

Jean L., aged 36, an alumnus of tlhe Ecole Normale Supéricure, has the agregation in physics. Hc is now a maitre-asistans (senior lecturer or assisrant professor) in one of the Paris universiries and livcs in the north-Dresrern suburbs. His faclier (an agrégé in grammar) was a lycée reacher and his grandfacher a primary teacher. His wife, a pharmacist's daughrer. is a denrisr. She reaches at the Paris Denral School and also runs her own practice.

## 'A Louis XIII convent mble from the Flea Market'

Preferring "sobriety' aind 'discrerion', Jean dishikes far cushions and heavy currains', and 'apartmenes done up by interior designers.' He is 'quire sensitive to the overall harmony of an interior':'1f you're lucky enough to come across a really fine piece of furnicure, you pur thar one piece in a. comer. Thar's all you need for a whole room.' 'Ar home, until re cently, we had cheap furnicure thar we bought when we martied. A quietly modern style thar wasn't unatractive. Veneered teak, quire chcap, bur now the chairs are giving up the ghosr . . ## A Young Executive Who 'Knows How to Live'

Michel R., an adverrising execurive working in a Paris agency, the son of the managing direcror of the Ftench subsidiary of a leading multinational corporation, srudied in a private Catholic secondary school in the 17rh arrondissement and then at the Paris Polirical Science Insticure; his wife, Isabelle, the daughrer of a provincial industrialisr, also wenr ro Sciences Po and works for a weekly news-magazine. He is 30, she is 28; they have rwo children. They live in Paris, in a modern five-roomed aparrmenr in the 1 5th arrondissement. They lke higs t  snu and cosy'. 'They have no inrerest in 'home-improvement' and have kept their aparrment as they found it. 'The decorarion is all the work of our predecessor. 1 didn't much like the green in the dining-room, it vas rather gloomy, bur we got used to it, and Iget bored working on the place 1 live in.' I hate thar beading on the doors, I'd like to ger rid of ir. The pseudo 16rh- or 18th-century vencering or whatever it is all over this modern aparrment is ghastly, 1 pur up aith ir but ir gers on my nerves,' says Michel, who has removed some of ir but \*couldn'r face the resr.'

## Model Executives

A rapid analysis of the 'exccurive opportuniries' advercised in Le Monde in the course of a single areck (in July 1973) is sufficient 10 identify the scl of characreristic fearures of the new breed of markering-oriented managers required by the new forms of busines organizarion:

Whether 'product manager' 'sales engineer" [these two rerms are in English in the original text], 'dcp uty sales direcror', 'assistant financial manager' or 'general sales manager"', he must above all be a 'negotiator' and a 'communicaror', and be

skilled in 'lop-level contaces: able to act with 'diplomacy"; adaptable to \*contacrs ar all levels'; 'accusromed to contaces wirh senior civil ser. vants, excellent negoriaror'; \*capaciry for high-level contacts'; \*top-level contacts and negoriation'; 'negotiarion with banks'; \*take charge ofrelalions with Government deparements, represent the firm on national negotiaring bodies'; 'rasre for conracrs and morivation': \*rasite for problem-solving and human relacions, highly arriculace':

and in internal negotiations, which means, for a head of sales management: 'an on-going co·ordination funcrion berween sales division and general management'; for a chief buyer, 'chis posicion entails full control of liaison berween a markering [English in original] deparcment and a producrion unir"; for a sales engincer, 'the negotiarions he will have ro conducr urill require an understanding atritude and the creativ. ity which his comperence justihes'; 'co-ordinator berween clienes, sales

schools, HEC, INSEAD. Ecole Institur Superieur des Affaires ISA), eneally liste toerbreras ud f sudy in an American university.

endowed wth te  ad tudes implied in working for multinational or srongly export-oriented frm (English absolurely indispensable: English vocabulary:marketing, 'merchandising' [last ro terms in English  cic., and Anglicisms: portunite' erc.);

the marketing auditor and the publicrelations auditor.'

The porttair of the modern manager, as drawn in 1973, seems r0 have changed recently, no doubt because the recession is creating conditions more favourable to the old style of management (chere is again a demand for the "eader of men'- someone who, as an informant put ir, 'can say no wirhour explaining' and an increased demand for produccion specialists and sales managers frained on the ground') and also because the engineering schools have reacted to the rise of the management schools (for example, the creation of the Institure for the Sciences of Action at Polyrechnique in 1977). According to a survey published in Le Nouvel Economiste (November 8, 1976), which questioned the personnel direcrors of 5,000 companies, firms still look for 'openmindedness' 'dynamism', 'capaciry to adapt and relace bilicy to ynthesize' and 'self-motivarion', bur they also insist on 'loyalty' (at Saint-Gobain) and ream spiri' (BSN and Oreal). Some 49 percent said they atrached importance to candidates' views on polirics and trade unions, 33 percent said they did not (18 percent did nor reply).

## Business Tourism

Reward seminars' and prestige seminars', as they sre callied in the native language, are parr of the range of hidden profits which modern firms offer their executives. 'Residential seminass' (chose which last longer than one day and take place away from company premises) provide business for one of the most flourishing industries. It is escimated that 25,000 such 'scminars' wert organiacd in 1973.) They involve the hotel chains which specialize in \*business rourism' (such as Novotel, Frantel, Sofirel, P.L.M., Méridien, Mercure and Morelleric); the agencies (such as Seminorel) which promote a ser of hocels specializing in seminars and conferences in exchange for 4 percent of curnover, consultancy firms (such as CEGOS or SEMA) and their social psychologists, who offer à la carte (sce the CEGOS 'caralogue' with irs 294 'formars' ac cares ranging from 200 to G00 francs per day) 'creativity seminars' togecher with 'facilira-

## I Have Met Happy Seminarists

If you're looking for the perfecr venue for a successful conference or seminar, the place you want is Mone d'Arbois near Megève (Haute Szvoie), in the hearr of the Mont Blanc Massif.

There I mer seminarists and conference-goers who were tanned, relaxed and happy to be there. They werethere to work, of course, but in a seccing thar helped them unwind and relax at the same cime.

On the work side, the Mone d' Arbois Hotel neers the parricular requirements of each firm. It's filly equipped with conference and committee rooms for groups from 20 to 200, audio-visual faciliries, simulrancous translation boochs. . . . The ar. rangement is always 'made to measure.'

There is easy access by air (Mont d'Arbois is 90 minutes from Paris by Air Alpes). by rail (night trains from Paris) and by road (a choice of routcs).

On the entertainment side, it's Paradise. Depending on the rime of you cn fantastic Mont Blanc Massif or enjoy one of the mosr beautiful golf courses in France. Ocher facilicies include tennis coures, an indoor swim. ming pool with a sauna and a gym.

For the less energecic, there are delightful walks in ourstanding countryside and the charming vil. lage of Megève with irs many amenicies. In the horel irself, gala evenings are organized on request, wich decorarions, a band and even visicing stars. Ar meal rimes, enjoy the impeccable cuisine and atrencive service offered by this rop-class hotel.

And how much does it cosc? The prices are accually very competitive, especiaily in September and December. Mr. Thommen or Mr.Ziegler ar che Mont d'Arbois Horel (rel. 50/ 21 25 03) will be gladto supply derails to help you compare.

One final point: it has been proved tha altitude stimulates the mental culies. Mont d'Arbois i t 1,00 me eyon in cop form.

When I go to Regional Development Council meerings, where I meet bankers and civil servants, I have to dress very correcry. For normal busiclose to decoration. To visit factories, I dlock in in a leather jacker and a polo-neck"' (italics mine).

The life-style of the new ethical avant-garde very directly expresses the asset structure which is rhe basis of its power and its conditions of exisrence. Executives in major national hrms, public or privare (a somewhat arrificial distinction, ar this level), or heads of large, modern, ofren mulcinacional companies, they are not attached ro a place like the proprietors of small local frms, local notables whose prestige is inseparable from a world of real interactions and personal representation. The new execucives look to a cente', thei headquarters, for directives and promotio large part of their prestige and power derives from academic qualifica-l guarantee, imbued with the economic-political culture taught in the po litical science institures or business schools and with the modernistc o nomic s pat whe nee  frm furure of

nary wod  whi en e ever mo wieyc  ann science' is developed within the discipline of economics, provides them with the sense of possessing an auchority of intellectual right over the conduct of sociery."Thus the opposition between the 'disinterested' culpied with the mundane interess of his practice, gives way, and not only among the new bourgeoisie, to the opposicion between the gratuitous, u ralis ulu the ntellectual ne eni rechnical culrure of 'modern managers', which scesitself as actionrienred bur irreducible to the trivialiry of mere 'practice'.

If old-style incellectuals continue to preserve an apparent monopoly over legitimare cultural practices, or ar least over the defnition of these practices, this is perhaps due to the inertia of the institutions of cultural production and diffusion (in particular, the educational system) and rhe hysteresis of habitus, which are continuously reinforced by the fact thar iy gerested culture, and consequencly the most legitimate of the marks of

all, her home must nor give the impression of being a place she just pases chrough, but look like a comfortable, rehned retrear, as lively as her own personality. She considers herself the first guest in her home. And as she hasn't much cime to give co it, shewantsit always to be ready to receive people, even when she's out all day long or goes off on long journeys. Result: a cosy, warm, congenial apartment which restibes to her presence and her preferences.

If she has a favourice colour, the décor makes it no secrer. 1f she trav. els a loz, every homecoming brings some improvement. if she loves painting or reading, the walls and shelves proclaim her tastes. Through her home, a woman whose job often requires her to adapt to other people's opinions, rediscovers the very feminine pleasure of saying, 'What I like is ...'

How does she entertain? Thar, of course, depends on the layout her home, whether she has a dining room or a fixed or fold-away diningbar. That, plus her domesric potencial, will decide whecher she'll give dinncr parties in the craditional style or ofter an clegant, humorous 'ready-to-serve' or \*selfservice' buffet.

As for the cooking, the work is ofren remote-controlled. norebook in hand, wich everything planned in advance. She knows all the rimesaving advancages of modern 1echniques; she has a well-rehearsed rp. erroirc of dishes thar can be prepared tlc night before; she's experr ar using the services of a carerer wichout depersonalizing her menus.

Thar's how coday's carecr-woman, a strategist of hospiraliry, combines charm with efficiency to give the illusion of being 100 percent devored ro her home!

## A 'Very Modest' Nurse

Mme. B., vhose parents had a small farm in the departmerir of the lot, is 48. She has worked ar the Sainr-Louis Hospical in Paris for almost twenty years. She 'used to love school' and would have liked to become a teacher, but had to leave a year afrer her CEP because her parents "couldn't afford to keep her". Divorced at 28, wirh rwo children, she had ro take a job in a hospital. While working, she studied to become a nurse. Her son, aged 26, is married; her daughter, aged 20. a biology student, lives wich her. Mme. B., who 'loves children', is 'horrifed by large families' (a lo of worry').

## I'm always shocked when I see a mistake'

The girls who work wich me don't speak properly, they make words feminine when they're masculine and vice versa, which proves they've no notion of grammar. It just shows you how primitive they are.' I'm always shocked when I see a miscake. Yesterday someone had wricrten examens amener with er . .. I'd be really ashamed if I made mistakes as obvious as thar... Ir is nor diffcul to find in these works the properties which, at a given momenc, predispose them to the treatment they receive from the new culrural intermediaries and their perit-bourgeois audience, when they aie not specially produced for this use. But it would be a mistake to locare in the works which enter into middle-brow culrure at a given moment the properries conferred on them by a particular form of consumption. As is shown by the fact that the same object which is today typically middlebrow—'average' (moyen)—may yesterday have figured in the most 're. lined' constellations of tastes and may be pur back there at any moment bhoasaker's cus which  capabl reabii the mostdiscredired object, the norion of an 'average' culture (culsure moyenne) is as fictitious as that of an 'average',universally acceptable language. What makes middle-brow culture is the middle-class relation to culure—misraken identity, misplaced belief, allodoxia Equally, ne must avoid treating this objectively and subjectively 'unhappy' relation in substantialist fashion, alchough it always becrays itself, in the eyes of the dominanr, by the most inconresrable and objective indices of a manner and mode of acquisition (such as, nowadays, the typically 'recordh eras  usical reference ha make da

## A Technician Who 'Tries to Get on'

Jacques C., aged 29, is a draughtsman in an enginecring consulrancy. At secondary school he studied in the rechnical scream and lefr school at 17 after obtaining the equivalent of che probationaly industrial r2ining diploma ('It isn't an examina. tion'). He started as a junior draughrsman, ar 450 francs a monch, in the firm where his facher was a senior rechnician. He was nor raken back afrer his milicary service, and he joined another lirm, scill as a draughtsman.

## 'Another five years to go at the CNAM'

He has changed firms several times: I would spend rwo years in a firm to learn all chere was ro bc learnr, and then I'd move on. That's how I've worked my way up.' in the last three years, he has specialized in building design. in 1966 he started to follow courses at the CNAM (Centre Naxional des Arts et Métiers)—'Acoileague gave me the idea.' As he had always wanred to do interior decoration (his father had been againsr ir when he was youger),  ecidedy n into archirecure' and began to rake courses in archirectural design': 'Thar's how I moved into the building crade . .' His sisrer, who is sudying to be an archiectural secetary, cold him about 'lots of architects' offices and the armosphere in chem.' So he has studied archicec. ture, the history o arcecure, construcrion ('chanks to which 1 got a place in an engincering consultancy') and has 'anorher five years to go' at the CNAM.

## To try to get on, achieve something'

The courses he is taking, to 'ry to gEr on,  achicve somerhing', absorb much of the little spare time he has. He leaves for work ar eight in the moming and gers back ar seven in the evening. Two or three evenings a week, and Saturday momings, he studies (as well as his CNAM courses, he is caking privace math lessons at home). So he doesn't have much rime for reading especially reading for pleasure': whar he does read 'rends to be rechnical or scientifc, and so on, ro ea somerhing. He is convinced thar he owes his posirion solety to his own meri, and tha for his salvarion he only has himself to rely on: 'Every man for himself 'A man's home is his castle. To concencrate his efforrs and reduce his costs, he will break the ries, even the family ties, which hinder his individual ascension. The bonds of solidarity which help to chain the (relarively) leasr deprived to the most deprived can make poverty an eternal vicius citcle. Taking off always presupposes a break, and the disowning offormer companions in misforrune is only one aspect of this. The olitary renegade has to reverse his whole rable of values, convert his whole attitude. Thus, substicucing a small family or a single child for the large family (which the negarive causes, such as inadequate mascery of the iechniques of birth control do nor encirely explain) means renouncing clie popular conceprion of family relations and the functions of the domen bndng no  e aticise extenmy ay . forbidden uxuries.

## The Declining Petite Bourgeoisie

The posicions whose numerical decline expresses cheir economic decline iv e c ng ple

A Baker's Wife Who Gets It Just About Right'

Mae ne e phanage. She went to school unril t CEP. Her husband frt worked a ng in a dairy. Then he was emploed as  baker or h. Twelveyears ago he ser up his own bakery business. They have one marned daugheer, aged 22. whoworks in a pharmacy.They own a smal! bakery (employing one other person), arhich they will soon be selling so as toacquireanocher small shop, but chis cime a busiess that is less demanding, 'less of a birid'. They have recently boughe a house in the suburbs of Grenoble; it has a living-room, dining-room, several bedrooms, kirchen and bathroom and is surrounded by a large, wellkept garden.

## 'I do like things to be clean'

Insi, eveyhing is brighly plh. I don'r want to b ur I do like things to be cle:an, so Ilike keeping my house nice and ridy, doing the dusting and sweeping. pethaps roo much, because if I was here all the time, char's all I'd be doingThe concern to easn t be ust our g neer conspicuous, is apparent in ever he worked forson of reward. e bad. Just an ordinary little

house. Nor that I'm boasting. If it had been a lirtle bit smaller, I would still have raken it. The face is, it's just abour right. Not superluxurious, no, bur nor just sun-ofthe-mill either.' 'We were brought up to be very economical. . . . Working the way we worked, we didn't have rime to spend money. . . We don'r have time to go our, so we're almost forced to save money. Often I say. "There are folks char have money and the time ro spend it; I've got the money but I haven'r gor the time." It's a sad fact bur it's true.'

## 'Something intermediate berween old and very modern'

Choosing their furnirure eventually bought from the chain-store Iévitan\~was 'a real headache'. I went round no end of shops . .. so as not to make a mistake. . .. I wanted somerhing that was right for my age because I can't really see a 50-year-old wich ulera-modern furniture. .. . 1 was looking for something intermediate berwecn old and very modern.' When buying the house, she preferred co \*cake her time' cather chan 'tush into it'. 'Some folks ger a house seraight away, but we wouldn't have been able to enjoy it, ir wouldn't have been any use ro us. We waiced cjuire a few years and char way I gor somerhing more comfortable.'

## 'Not the most ordinary, but not the most beautiful either'

In the living room—'it's nor the most ordinary furnicure in the world, but ic isn't the mosr beausiful either—I went for somerhing classic.' As for che big grey sercce, 'with thas shade, you can sir on it", 'you're not a slave to ir' (for fear of marking ir) 'and ar the same time it's quice comfortable.' She had to choose all chese things on her own; her husband had no time and no inclination to accompany her. 'He couldn's have cared less, he just said "Ger pharever you like." She looked for somerhing \*chat would go wich the sryle of the rooms. I don't claim to be very comperent bur you do have to respect a cerrain style in the rooms.'

## Those things are worth a bir now they're cleaned up'

On the wall chere is a painting given to her by her brother-in-law and one she bought from a 'picture artisr'. 'I would like to have more paincings but I can's afford ic' She chinks she might also like to listen to records bur has never bought any and cannot imagine herself doing so. Her sense of economy, the refusai to wasce anyching, has led her to 'rescue' knick-knacks for her house. 'I've gor lots of trinkers and odds and ends chat I found in aunes' and uncles' arrics. They were terribly tamished and rusty bur I polished them up. All those shings are worth a bir now they're cleaned up. . . . When 1 tescued them, no. body would have bothered to pick them up, they were so dirry.

## 'I don't fike showing off'

Her home means a lor to her. When she has money co spare, shc would rather 'spend ir on furniure or somerhing for the house', buy currains or a carper thar she can 'keep for a long cime', racher than a dress rhar will be our of fashion nexc year or jewellery thar she will hardly ever wear. 'Some people are constancly buying new clothes. I don't feel che need. Sometimes you end up buying things you never

even wear. You lke te loo ihop and then how, overnighr, you decide you don't like them any more. Take s v e  you wear them once for hour or rwo and then you chink, "No, they hurt roo much, I woli't wear them", and chen they stayin the box. I'm sure I'm not the only ersn thar happens S li re jewellery old jewellery, b does not wear whar she owns."I don't like showing of, purring all my jewellery on; people would say "Look ar her, dressed up in all her jewellery!" 1 don't like flauncing my wealth, if thar's the word for ir.

## Just trim to tidy myel

She never splurges' on clothes: not one to spend a lot on my wardrobe.'Anyway, 'fashion is consrandy changing .. wharever you do, you can never keep up with is.'Insread, se goes ore or classic c. She is not sure wherher to accep a wedding inviration: 'I'm nor keen because it means buying a lor of ex. pensive new chings and chen you only wear them once'She occasionally goes to the hairdresser's and te gards it as a 'chore': 'just a ttim to cidy myself up.' In che country, where she spent her childhood, 'you didn't sit for hours in front of a mirror putting make-up on. Normally, Madame D. does nor do much real cooking just for the o of us'; but when they have guests. she enjoys cooking traditional thingsuiche oraine. grain d no l ors  oss suff maroes.

## 'People who chrow their money away'

She gets nl h who have the same rasce

se ies de ent people. withou being, shall we say, mean' Being 'quire economical y ger on wirh people who throw heir money away She Cano n derstand people who 'haven't tw penns t er n t spendi ofpeople who sint themselves leas; ofen it's middle-class people who won' deny chemselves anythg They and buy fancy ckes or  botle of good wine whenever they feel like it and when the moneyruns our they pur it on their accounr.' She extends the same disapproval to those 'who don's know hoa' to manage their budger' and who ask for credit cowards the end of thic monch—meaning, no doubr, although she is nor explicir, vorking-clas people.

## Cheerful programmes you don't have to think about'

She has nor been to the cinema for ar least cen years', and says she doesn't have time to read either a daily paper or weekly magazines, like Paris Maich or jours de France, which customers somerimes leave behind in the shop: 'a lo of pages with not much on them and a lor rs S arc vision a little, bur nor too much ainlySndays, uever cen ar nighr. She is not 'a Ty fanatic' but likes 'cheerful programmes' thar 'you don't have to hibou lly ainment so long as the producer doesn't ty to be clever' Nowadays grame; is was much berrer te way it used to be' She refuses all formal experimentacion and special effeces, and has nor liked Avercy's recerit experimental produccions: 'I don't like those cut-up chings ar all, where you sce a head, then a nose, then a leg. lt jusr seems plain silly to me; I musr be old-fashioned... First you sce a singer all drawn our, three merres rall, then he's got arms (wo merres long. Do you find chat funny? Oh, I just don't like it, ir's scupid; I don't see the poinr of distorting things.' Bur she does like to warch a 'cradirional' singer, 'a singer who just sings. who sings normally, who's normal size and nor all dis-. rorred.'

## 'My husband doesn't like hotels'

Every year, they go on holiday in cheir caravan for cwo or chree weeks. Two or three times they have been to a caravan camp sire on the Riviera and last year chey srayed beside a lake in the Grenoble region. Unril they got the caravan, they did not go on holiday; her husband 'doesn's like horels at all, or restauranrs.' On holiday, her htisband plays bowls and cards and 'makes a lot of friends.'She doesn't like doing noching, so she relaxes and knics or makes capestry: 'ir's a nice hobby, che rime goes quicker.' They spend a bic of time on che beach and drink pastis with friends. Aparr from che summer holidays, their work is so demanding char they have no time to go out. Her husband, who srarts work at nine every Sunday evening, carches up on his sleep Sunday afremoon. Ar most, chey go our 'once a year, on Easrer Monday or Whir Monday. Because ir was a public holiday, wc used to shur up shop for rwo days, Sunday and Monday.'

## The Executant Petite Bourgeoisie

members are distribured, in respect of diachronic properties, from the geoisie in heir ethical and aestheric choices, to the youngest, who, especially when possessing high qualifications, are similar to the mobile elcments of the new petite bourgeoisie. Everything takes place as if the basic disposition which characterizes the fraction as a whole were systematically transformed as a function of age and social origin, from an optimistic progressivism among the rising young to a pessimistic, regressive conservatism among the oldest.

It is among the youngest members of the occupations offering the most secure future, such as the junior executives and ofice workers, and more especially among those who originate from the working class and have only moderate qualifcations (BEPC or baccalauréat) thar one fnds the most developed form of the asceric disposirions and devorion to culture associated wich the ambition to pursue by further accumulation of cultural capital a rise made possible by an initial small accumulation. # A Nurse Who Lives with Passion'

h coll Fo Uniereal Sdent Residence Paris. Her - th  aivil ervan nd r   Ofice er. Sh Ives alone in a oneroom apartment with a smal enance-hall. Ion cains no furniture—no table, no chairs, no shelves. ## 'I love being creative'

She is eager to express her personality and scizes every opporruniry to 'create' somerhing personal: "1 love drawing .. . everyone in the family does drawing: my sisrer draws, my father used to draw.' Mainly, though only episodically, she does black-and-white portrairs of people she likes: '1 enjoy colour bue for me it's nor the imporcant rhing in a drawing'; the most imporrant things are the curves and then, well, the expression, capturing someone's ex\* pression . . . rhe pleasure comes when I'm doing the drawirig, larer it's of no significance.' She learne how to make enamelware: first from books, which her aunt gave her togerher with a kiln when she was 15; later she made enamelware with her sisrer and friends at the municipal youthclub.

## 'People who never look outside chemselves'

Her only make-up is an invisible foundation cream; her hair is thinned and cuc medium-lengrh. The first quality she looks for in her friends 'is joy. I like healthy people, who are glad to be alive.' 'ilike people who are at ease with themselves, perhaps because ['m nor always ar ease wrh myself. 1 think that whar makes people carry on liv. ing is always being able to hopc for something, never acceping chings as chey are. . . . Thc people I don't like, i can't accept because they seem ro me . . . empry. 1 mean they're dull, they have no rasre, no passion. Perhaps "nopassion" is t0o strong; I mean theydon't want anything, they don't seem to fcel any. thing, they never look outside themselves. They don't really live.

'They're imprisoned by a whole environment. They can't ger out of ir and don's even realize they could ger our of it. There are quire a lor of them among my parienrs and rhe people i work wich. People who are perhaps coo dependenr on material satisfaccions, on the material things society can offer them .. I can't say I don'r like chem, bur I'm not interesred in them. They don't interest me'

## 'Doing something wich my body'

She worships 'nature vei that is natural in people, in the streer', and loves 'nature in Paris, the woods and Alowers.' For a whole year she practised 'physical expression' with the Montreuil thearre group: 'Doing something wich my body . . . performing movements, gestures, that aren'c necessarily theacrical, that express my body—chat's somethingI enjoy.' On holiday, she shuns campsites the'e too a nized nowadays, almost hotels'); she cannor spend more chan a week at the seaside 'doing nothing': 'Going to the beach, sun-barhing, the occasional swim . . . afrer (wo days of thar, I'm bored.' She hich-hikes to Greece, fcaly or the Balearic Islands: 'I love ravelling that way partly because it's so different from what I do the rest of the year, a different life-style . . . but also in order to live with a bir of insecurity--thar's exciting—and to meer diff erent people, be able to communicace.' Iast year she rook pare in che restoration of an abbey in the Rouergue region, and in her spare cime she has worked wich an archaeological ream excavating a sice in Eure-et-Loire. Every other Wednesday (she works one Wednesday out of t wo) she arrends a course at the Ecole Pratique des Haures Erudes 'roger a bit of hyel  ave rions of hisrory and archaco ology).

## We used to choose a theme, talk about it and then crea

## 'I don't go just because its cheaper'

## Boris Vian and lots of chings abouc him

Thus, the arr craftsmenin the old sense-upholsterers, wrought-iron workers, cabinermakers, picture-framers, goldsmiths, jewellers, glders orengravers, trained in technical schools and very close to small caftsmen and their values—have been joined in the last fifreen years or so by makers of jcaellery, printed fabrics, ceramics or hand-woven clothes, wich a higher level of general education, often Parisian and of bourgeois origin, who are closer in life-style ro the culcural intermediaries. Similarly, among the secreraries and nurses, some, originating from the lower or middle classes, are very close to the junior administrative execurives, while others, younger, often Parisian and of bourgeois origin, present all the features of the new occupations. In general, the indeterminacy of the new or renovared occupations means that the heterogeneity of the agents' trajectories is patricularlly marked. One can almost always distinguish rwo groups sepavrues ecessary iorder o.

Those members of the new petite bourgeoisie who originate from the upper classes and who, for lack (most often) of educational capiral, have had to reconvert into the new occupacions such as culcural intermediary or art crafcsman have had fewer years of schooling rhan the average for rherefore possess a very great cultural capital of familiariry and a social capiral of 'connections'. They manifest the highest competence within the middle classes and incline rowards a syscem f choices very similar to that of the bourgeoisie: the Art of Fugue, che Concerto for the Left Hand, the Firebird Suite, the Four Seasons, Goya, Braque, Breughel, Jacques Douai, che Modern Art Museum, ancique shops and che Flea Marker, a 'harmonious', 'discreer', 'studied' incerior, 'rehned, 'artistic', 'stylish' friends, 'intellectual' fims. Salvatore Giuliano, Exterminating Angel, The Trial, or among the comedies, The Suitor (see figure 18). Their ambivalent relationship with the educarional system, inducing a sense of complicity with every form of symbolic dehance, inclines them to welcome all the forms f culture which are, provisionally at least, on the (lower) boundaries of legitimate culture—jazz, cinema, strip carroons, science fiction—and to flaunt (for example) American fashions and models— jazz, jeans, rock or rhe avant-garde underground, which is their monop oly—as a challenge t legitimace culture; but they often bring into these regions disdained by the educational establishment an erudire, even academic' disposiction which is inspired by a clear incencion of rehabiliration the culcural equivalenc of the restoration strategies which define their oc cupational project.

The fundamenral proposirion thar che habirus is a virrue made of necessity is never more clearly illustrared than in the case of the working classes, since necessity includes for them all thar is usually meant by the word, thar is, an inescapable deprivarion of necessaty goods. Necessity imposes a raste for necessity which implies a form of adapration to and consequently acceptance of the necessary, a resignation to the inevirable, a deep-seared disposirion which is in no way incompatible wirh a revolurionary intention, although it confers on it a modalicy which is not that of inrellecrual or artistic revolts. # Foreman Who Has Always Worked for Other People'

Mr L, aged 61, starred work as an appn i enc i he age of foueen and aa. He became a skilled worker and is now a foreman. He 'deals wich passeger rolgstock His wieaged 52, has never worked. She completed secondary school and would like to find a job. They have had four children; the eldest is a compurer programmer, the second a Dominican monk, the third used ro be a personal assisranr bur sropped work when she married, and the youngest is studying for his baccalaurear. They live in a municipatsiyle Aar in Grenoble.

## 'You have to know what you're doing'

Mr. L. has done a lot of work on the apartment: 'You can sce if you look; chere used to be a room there and a room here. Because there were so many of us ir was really cramped, so I rook the wall down, which means we've gor more space, we can enterrain more people, especially with the whole family.. We need a lot of room, especially when my son has his friends round, all singing anddancig' Mos  t - ou Ts ee they lived for a number of years. ## 'I koew how to make good usc of it'

Their home is 'no museum'; the or namenrs and vases are 'nor just dust traps The varius objecs adorning the aparrmenr are mostly presenrs from the children or friends, or have been 'rescued', and they all have their 'use. Madame L. will nor buy anyching uncil she 'has already found a place for it.' 'Thar vase now, I needed a vase. I wanted a vase because I wanted flowers, and when i was asked whar I wanted, 1 said "a vase" and they boughr it for me because I knew how to make good use of ir.' His children 'know perfecty well there's no point in buying things thar I won'r use or that I'll pur away in a sideboard. Everything has co have a place for it ... otherwise, I don't like to have things just to put them away' They make most of their purchases in supermarkers (Carrefour, Record) or deparmenr srores (Nouvelles Galeries): 'I don't like window-shopping, I go srraight to a big supermarker and you find everything there; you can choose wichour going round a dozen shops,' Mr. L. explains. On holiday he always starrs by 'checking our the marker': 'I'm interested in the price 1 pay and the quality of whar I ger. 1f you buy wich your eyes closed, hey'll fo you  with anychig

## 'Now, I'll be glad to work'

Recencly they became the owners of a lirrle house in the mountains ourside Grenoble. 'Some friends-—he was an engineer'--invired them to rake ir over several years ago. In the lase three years, they have done a lor of work on it: \*Everyching was rotren. I was walking around upsrairs and I fell chrough the floor There were no riles lefr on the roof: the rain came in on all sides.' Mr. L. has done all che repair work himself: \*You know, I srarted as an apprentice on the railways, and an apprentice had to learn everything. You got a chree-month course on sani tation, chree monchs on elecrro-welding and so on. And you always remember your trade, to some exrent.' They incend ro make this house cheir mam home. 'Now, I'll be glad to work, now thar it's my house;I'm nor working for orher people I'm fed up wich working and being exploired.'

## 'Learning to ski at 42'

He is 'very fond of cold weacher, snow, but nor skiing': I'm afraid of hurting myself. 1 go robogganing with my grandchildren. You see, I had an accident in my brother-inlaw's car in Tunisia . . . and I injured my wrist. You only have to look at ir to see the srare it's in. . That's why I prefer tobogganing. I can sic up and chere's no danger of falling.(His wife 'started leaining to ski ar 42, because of che children.... We used tro go our wirh them, we didn'r want to spend the whole day sitring in a café or freezing in che car.") When he was young,he 'did a lor of sport, especially foorball.'

## 'You just have to like chem'

He somerimes does the cooking: 'When my wife's away or with her siscers and I'm here on my own, I'd rther do my own cooking than go and car ar my morher's or one of my siscers-in-law's.' When she

wans  ive someone wife looks up a menu in her recie books.' I don't alrogether agree with my children when they say. "You should cook someching you like" I say. "You ofren do things i  that yo don't li  s wirh, bur you just have ro like them"  buy rable wine ar Carefour; it's cheaper there. It's eleven proof and it cosrs one franc fry. botle i'  an five, I think.'

## 'Always someching to keep me busy'

They go camping for their holidays. 'Ar the seaside, we go swimmingI like looking for shell. fish . .. In the mounrains, I go looking for mushrooms, or snails. There's always somerhing ro keep me busy.'And his wife adds cha last suminer he was a bit disap. poinred because there was nohing to go looking for ... He doesn't like being idle, be always has ro ind something to do. In fact thae's why we go camping. He couldn's really see himself sitting around in hotels and resraurans, whereas when we go cainping, he can keep himself busy wich the shopping, and a bit of swimming, of course, and all things thar have to be done And then we generally have a look round the surrounding area..  Whenwe did Italy, we wenr all over the place looking ar all the old sires, all the archaeological things. My husband doesn't enjoy chat as much as I do Peronally, I belong  an adult cation group and so thar sort of ching incereses me.

## Circuses, games, quizzes

On celevisonhe watches foorball marches, cycling, all sorts of spor.

## Culture and Politics

Perhaps the most radical approach to the problem of policics is to ask of it the quescion chat Marx and Engels raise in relation to art. Having analysed the concentration of the capacity for arcistic production in the hands of a few individuals and the correlative (or even consequent) dispossession of the masses, they imagine a (communist) sociery in which 'there are no painters bur at most people who engage in painting among other chings',' and in which, chanks to the development of che productive forces, the general reduceion of working cime (chrough an overall decrease and an equal discribution) allows 'everyone sufficient free time ro take part in the general affairs of sociery—sheorelical as well as practical: 'There are no policicians bur at most people who engage in politics among orher accivicies': here as elsewhere, a uropia is scientifically appearances and forces one to bring to light che presupposicions of the usual order. Whar nee obe i e y not peral n The opinion poll, by urging all its respondenrs, wichout distinccion, produce a 'personal opinion'—an incention underlined by al the according-to-yous', 'in-your-views' and 'whar-do-you-personally-thinks'inthe questionnaires—or to choose, by cheir own means, unaided, between several pre-formulared opinions, implicitly accepts a policical philosophy which makes policical choice a specifically political judgement, applying political principles co answer a problem thar is presented as policical, and which credirs everyone wich nor only the right bur also the power to pro duce such a judgement. A social history of che norion of 'personal opin ion' would no doubr show thar this eighreenth-century invention is coored in the rationalist belief that the faculry of 'judging well, as Descr calledthar  iscerning good froma truth fro rin  n sentacive bodics).

The act of producing a response to a questionnaire on politics, like vog or nother lev paricipation, reading a party newspaper (journal d'opinion) or joining a party, is a particular case of a supply meeting a demand. On one side is the field of ideological production, a relatively autonomous universe in which amidsr comperition and conflicr, che inscruments for chinking the social world are creared and where, through chis process, the field of the politically chinkable, or, to puc ic another way, the iegitimare problemaric, is defined.'On the other side are social agerts, occupying differen posicions in theheld of class relations and defined by a grearer or lesser specific political comperence--a grcater or lesser capacity to recognize a policical question as political and to trear it as such by respondmg to it politically, i.e., on the basis of specifically political principles ( cacher chan ethical ones, for example). This capacity is inseparable from a more or less strong feeling of being competent, in the full sense of che word, chat is, socially recognized as encicled to deal wich policical affairs. to express an opinion abour chem or even modify their course. In facr, there is reason to suppose thar comperence in the sense of technical capacity (policical culture) is correlared wich competence in the sense of a socially recognized capacity, ascribed by and ascribing status—the opposite of which is borh imporence and objective uiv      - terest me').

## Selective Democracy

' c more remote from experience, more abstract and detached from ordinaly realicies in cheir content and phrasing (and also, but secondarily, when chey have only recencly appeared in the field of ideological production) and when they require more insisrently a response produced on the basis oecifcally polical princples  demntha is percive in tee syntax and vocabulary of the question).

Everything takes place as if the most 'legitimate'agents, that is, those most comperent in both senses, wete and felt that much more legitimated—i.e., inclined and called upon to express an opinion—the more 'legitimate' the problem posed. Thus, one finds that those who cannot reply to the question of their political allegiance or preference (indicating the party to which they feel closest) are those who are also most inclined to leave the other questions unanswered-especially when the question posed is clearly located in the register of professional policics. The incerviewees who fall into the non-aligned 'grey area' in the SOFRES survey do nor reply much less often (81 percent) than those who idencify wich the extreme left (91 percent), the left (90 percent), the centre (86 percent), the right (93 percent) or the extreme right (92 percent), when asked whether France should help 'poor countries'. But when asked whether France should favour councries wich a 'democratic regime', they reply significancly less ofren (57 percent) than those idenrifying wich the extreme lefc (76 percent), the left (67 percent), the centre (75 percent), the right (70 percent), or the exceme right (74 percent) (SOFRES. 971).

## Class Consciousness

To srarr with, when I gor married, I had no definire opinions, I was from Brirtany, see, 1 certainly felt there were problems and, well, 1 felt it, but I couldn't define them. I married a Communisr, wichout realizing ir. At firsr, you know, when 1 had just arrived in the house, when I saw L'Humanité Dimanche, I was furious. You see, 1 didn't know che paper. for monchs and years I didn't know abour ic. And then, litcle by liccle, you see, I was led to recognize, you see, char maybe its atritudes were a little bic hard, but the fact was, they were righr. My husband was a trade-union man, so I was dealing wich a frade unionist, and then I had one, rwo, three children, then, you see, I wenr thtough the diffculies of life like everyone, T bad parches, because I had to look after m mother as well as evey. thing else. And my husband's a worker, right, so I knew aboutth workers' problems. And then I really made up my mind at the rime of the Algerian war, you see, I real. ized thar monstrous things were being nehr against. We'd always choughe tha that war was wrong and thar was really when I understood what the Party stood for. Especially when there was the massacre ar Charonne, remember? Well, that completely revolted me, because you see, I'd made my husband stay at home tha evening, otherwise he mighr have been chere.'

also social origin. I•

Such a reading—overemphasizing the unequal distribution of political capital—which would be supported by all the appearances, would do no more than record rwo contrasting and complementary representawhich makes technical comperence (as defined by rechnocrars)the pre condicion for access to 'policical responsibilicies' or 'responsible' policical choices, and the complementary representation, based on the sense of incomperence and imporence, which condemns the economically and cultuallye ecepel another way of overestimating the orher classes. Like every recording of 'dara' ar face value, this endorsement of appearances would have the ef. fect of preventing inquiry into the objective bses of these represencasions. and, more precisely, would prevent one from looking into the realicy of che division of policical labour for the trurh contained in these representarions of the division of labour.

In fact, here as elsewhere, the relationship berween realicy and representations is established chrough the dispositions which are the incernalized fo of the probabiliies asociat with a iven position in thedivison of labour. 'Technical' competence depends fundamentally on social comperence and on the corresponding sense of being enticled and required by starus to exercise this specific capacity, and cherefore to possess it. ln ocher words, to undersrand the relarionship berween educational capiral and che propensicy to answer policical questions, ir is nor sufficient to consider the capaciry to undersrand, reproduce, and even produce policical discourse, which is guaranteed by educational qualifications; one also l principles."

Every policical 'choice' takes into account, simulraneously, the guarantor's personaliry and what he guarantees. The delegate is both a representacive who expresses the already expressed opinions of his mandarors (he is 'bound' by a programme, a sort of explicic contract of delegation) and an agent who, following his internal programme—or the specific interests associated with his position in the held of ideological production—more than his declared programme, expresses as yet unformulated, implicit or porential opinions which he thereby brings into existence. ## Dispossession and Misappropriation

aent,or even ituoso  oliical niess, e avoid having recourse to different modes of production—and the more so, the less the sicuation to which he has to respond is polirically conscitured. Other things being equal, the more clearly the problem posed is recognized as policical, the more frequent is the use of the two specifcally political modes of production, proxy and autonomous production. They are also more frequent, in respect of explicitly political problems, when policical consciousness is higher. In other words, they depend on the ser of factors (sex, education, social class etc.) which govern the propensicy to answer the most specifically policical questions (as opposed to the propensity to abstain).

Or problems mentioned as policical is very low among the educationally unqualifed, grows steadily wich level of education and rises to rwo-thirds among chose wich the baccalaunéat or a licence. (No doubebecause ir was a quesion of 'problems', che variations by education are greater here than for the apticude to sce various actions as policical.As might be expecre t variacions by educational capital are greater in relacion ohoseproblems thos which are rarely posed ourside the feld of ideological production, pa  olii e ment, workers' parcicipation, wage raises or the price of meat.

This means thar che relationship berween social class and political opinions varies by social class, i.e., according to the mode of opinion production most frequent in that class. The probabilicy of producing a policical response to apolicically consticuted question rises as one moves up thel herarchy nd he ierarchy  ces nd qualans). w ca  e y     - p senior executives and professions 16.1 percent ). There is a vcry close correlacon with declare poliical allegiance in the specifcally policical congovernmenr's foreign policy: Communist Parry [PCF} 48.7 perceo, Sol . l cpicale 'rviro te cateoy, precisey n respe o te degree of politicization.

Furthermore, the gap berween the capacity to produce a minimal re. quesin r a least t grasp hespeciically poliialnicn duction of specifically political problematics) leads to the imposicion of a appropriated. The respondents are dispossessed of the meaning of their response whenever they choose one of the answers offered to a question which they do not have the means of asking, and so allow an opinion which is cocally alien ro them (even if it happens to 'express' them) co be treated as their own opinion; or whenever they respond, not to the quescion asked, bur to the one they chemselves have had to produce to try to appropriare the initial question. (This work of re-appropriation, which is almost always manifested by a recranslacion into another language, so that 'rationalizarion of budgerary options' becomes 'not wasting money for example, is almost invariably ignoted by policical scientists, either ac the levelo bservation or t the ie o cog.)

One example (see table 30) will suffice to show the imposition effect-and the allodoxia whic resuls. This is a question n he poliial infuenc the business world' (les affaires), in which we incidentally see che use of (wo very common rhetorical devices: the imposicion of presupposicions— here, 'in evcry councry', an anodyne phrase which insinuares the whole conservarive philosophy of history (always and cvcrywhere the same')—and the false symmecry which allows a highly improbable, almost absurd answer to exisr, under the guise of giving equal chances to all replies. To this should no doubt be added the effect of neurralizarion and euphemizarion, tending ro discourage any indecent' expression of indignation or revolt. The question itsclf was only devised in order to legirimate (by another false symmetrythe asking o another question, chis time definitely part of the dominant problemacic: 'And in your view, is che infuence of the trade unions too grear, about right, or roo small?" It can be seen that the propor tion who consider the influetice of the business world' too great rises as c ata iehierarcy  ucacinal level a Precise ce omanual worke ugie nduce  busies excive while 52 peicent retreat into abstention or neurrality, with a considerable proporrion acrually judging' that infuence too small. The retranslarion which the least competent respondents have to perform in order to begin to appcopriare a long and complex question on a 'policical-sciencific' issue, Often resulting in misunderstandings, is seen in anorher case (table 31). where the least equipped have no choice but to abstain or to answer in rerms of cheir image of Mirrerrand. Herc too, a neurralization effecr is achieved through stylistic effecs as subele as the 'Mr.' before Mirrerrand, or theuse of an academic verb such as préconiser (advocate); the expression 'socialism of the possible' iself belongs to the regiscer of high policics.

Misappropriatrion almosr inevirably occurs whenever the question in vires rwo different readings, when the uninitiared cannor answer it without bringing ir down from the specifcally political plane, where it is siruated by the professionals who produce it and who will analyse or comment on the resulrs, ro the plane of ordinary experience, to which the unconscious schemes of the ethos can be directly applied. Thus the politically 'incomperent' have every likelihood of placing themselves in the camp of the champions of the moral order and the social order, and even of appearing more conservarive in this area than the conscious defenders of the social order, whenever they are led to apply the categories of their class erhos to problems already constitured polirically at rhe level of the field of ideological production. It should scarcely be necessary to point u tha if the members f the dominant class are more innovating' in domestic morality but more 'conconcerned with the economic and political order and with class relations h e prensity to adopt nvatin'or 'revolurinary' posiins vari verse ratio wich the degree to which the changes in question affect the hasis of their privilege. Thus, to take just one example, senior execurives may ap pear more 'liberal' in their view of immigrant workers than manual work-" ers, who are more directly concerned by their competition: these executives much more often say that 'France is not doing enough to help foreign workers find accominodarion' (67 percent compared with 48 percent), 'to give them training' (52 percent and 36 percenr) or 'make them welcome' (45 percent and 33 percent). Bur their liberalism is less marked when measures which might have consequences for themselves,are mentioned: 41 percent of the senior executives and professionals consider that 'France is not doing enough to give decent wages' to immigrant workers, as against 43 percent of the manual workers and 51 percent of the office workers and junior executives; 48 percent consider thar 'France should reduce or stop is aid to under-developed countries' as against 3 percent of manual workers and 35 percent of offce workers and junior execurives (SOFRES, La France, l'Algérie et le Tiers-Monde, February 1971).

It only recognizes the questions which the dominant class ask chemselves about the groups which are problems for them, and ignores the quesrions these groups ask (and the problems they have).

The mos deprived do not, stricly speaking, know what question they are answering, and do nor possess the interests and disposicions thar e order. They cherefore respond nor to the question thar is actually asked, bur to a quescion they produce from their own resources. i.e., from the practical principles of their class echos. When the questions posed are in cal reaction is most clearly secn—especially in those whose posicion in al l ievoluinay sanc  decini p bourgeos w ial i    oa cendant petit bourgeois, who are convinced of their right to settle scores with a society which has not sufficiently rewarded their merits. This simple reversal of pretension, the disposicion of groups condemned to occupy subjectively a position which is not objectively theirs, is characteristic of all those who condemn the established order only because it recognizes them less than they recognize it, in their very revolt, and cannot recognize in them the values it recognizes offcially. This is why enlightened conservatism, which is always ready to accept or even initiate changes in every area which docs touch the foundations of the social order,4 finds, on both right and left, the foils it needs in order to presenc itself as a vanguard.

## Supply and Demand

aalle en politically marked newspapers and magazines. However, the inhereit ieng what eading  neapermeans r te iferent cgo readers, since it may have nothing in common with the functions com mnlyued  ithosgned  e odue .

Fi Lib ciatns,000 n360,000 repecivel a iro,  ent n  a n u, L'Axrore declare themselves to be lefc-wing vorers), 60 percent and 64 perc  ev eh  pap yh wu rea he pa t al p c own, and a high proportion decline to pronounce on their objectiviry or their stances in the election campaign. Bur above all, only a small proporrion of their readers (24 percent for Le Parisien Libere and 29 percent for France-Soir—as against 37 percent for Le Figaro and 42 percent for L' Aurore) say they are in complere agreementwith the opinions expressed by these papers, whereas a large proporrion (40 percent and 50 percent respectively) conierthem more righr-wing than themselves. I cn be seen that the spe cifically political effect of a newspaper is not measured by the polirical oricntation of its specifically political discourse as evaluated in the held of ideological producrion, stil less by the number of column-inches directly devored to poliics, but by the relacionship the readers have to the paper whose polirical message they may ignore and whose most imporrant polirical acrion may be to give no importance to politics.

It would seem thar the lower the readers are sicuared in the social hierarchy, the more independent their policical opinion is of the opinion explicicly professed by their newspaper. Buying a paper like Le Parisien Libere or France-Soir (or even L'Aurore) is, for manual workers or clerical workers who vore Socialist or Communist (and who no doubt make up the major. ity of the Communist or Socialist vorers among those papers' readerships, ie, 10 percent and 20 percent of the readership of the former and 9 percent and 29 percene of the readership of the latter), an act which, although it is ieylcl i  u jon' implies no afiliacion, no political delegation. Le Parisien Liberé, which dvo . percent   a  r  ins .6 eren  L Mo,  o par or r as a sports paper which, unlike L'Equipe, provides general and anecdoral news as well for the same price.

The relacive independence of the re ders' policical opinions from their newspapers' political rendency chus scems from the fact thar, unlike a polirical party, a newspaper ofters informarion char is not exclusively political (in the narrow sense normally given to this word). Thus, for some major parties uch as the present-day Socialist Patcy) and some major publicacions, a racional management of compericion wichin rhe production unit, which then funccions as a field, can be the means of supplying the different fractins o readers or electors (for example, in the case of Le Monde, the different fractions of the dominant classwith diversifed products adjusted ther dierent  ve psigexpation, wihou  consc tention of doing so.

Bu n dnana nclel woke—exc cized of them, who read L'Humanité or another farleft paper.—pra

i as  e whc diffrent in nature from the interes which those members of te domialkv called 'general news', the appointment of civil servants to ministerial cabinets or of technocrats to 'think-ranks', elections ro the Académie française or receptions ar the Elysée Palace, the power struggles wichin te lial r   uces wih ae or frm, nor to mention the diary ofsocial events or the ist of admissions to the grandes écoles. Only in bourgeois conditions of existence, around bourgeois dinner tables, do proper names of general interest—the name of the Minister of Finance or his chief adviser, the director of Schlumberger or the chairman of a prize jury and so forth-refer to familiar individuals, known and frequented, belonging, like neighbours and cousins in the space of a village, to the world of mucual acquaintance (which gocs a longway to explain why reading Le Monde is a sine qua non for entry to the beau monde). One forgers that the dominant class is defned precisely by he c tha ihas a paricular interes naffairs  eeal incerest' becausc the parricular incerests of its members are parcicularly bound up with chose aff airs.

But this is only one of the many reasons for questioning the semiscintific opposition berween 'news' and \*views', or berween the so-called sensacional newspaper and the 'journal of reflecrion'Behind the reading o     u policics. H la in  hc  i ela i sindications  poliical posiions from tevariationsi cd cultural dividing-line berween the working classes—who, apart from local papers, read almost exclusively omnibus papers—and the mida e  cal worke w  mo p but also more righr-wing papeis (i.e., more ofren La Croix, Le Figaro and Le Monde less ofren L'Humanité or L'Equipe ). One no doubt sees here the combined effects of very different work milieux, the workshop and the ofice, and also of types of schooling which tend to reinforce pre. exiscing differences—technical craining, which encourages practices and incerests close to those of other manual workers, and secondary schooli whh, by  leas sli iain  ppls nto lime cl and its values, introduces a break wich the popular world view.

To verify this, one only has to observe that foremen, although they have distinctly higher incornes and a geographical distribution vely similar t0 char of the clerical workers (who are onty a lirtle more concentrated in Paris), have much lower readership rates for the naxional dailies and the weeklies {18.5 percent and 28.4 percent respectively, as against the clerical workers' 41.4 percent and 43.2 percent); foremen more often read the omnibus papers and, among the weeklies, L'Humanité Dimanche, whereas the clerical workers slightly more often read Le Figaro, Le Monde and La Croix, and. among the weeklies, La Vie and especially Le Nouvel Observateur. There is every rcason to think that those whom chis dividing-line separares are op posed in other respecrs, parcicularly as regards their attitudes to religion, the trade unions and the political patties, with, on the clerical workers' side, a higher proportion of practising Catholics, Force uvriere unionists and Socialists (or PSU, or extreme-left) vorers, but also Gaullists, and on che manual workers' side, a higher proporrion of agnostics or non-pra.ctising Catholics, CGT unionists and Communist voters. "These opposirions are themsclves an integral part of life-styles linked to different social trajectories and diferent working conditions (for example: differences according to how long the lincage has been urbanized and proletarianized, according to the size of the firm etc.).

The boundary betwcen the industrial working class and the peasantry is also marked by the probabilicy—much higher among industrial workers—of seading a national newspaper, especiallyL'Humarite, and by the fact chat agricultural workers almosr always ncad only Catholic weeklies (Le Pilerin, La Vie) whereas industrial workers rcad a greazer variery of papers. As one moves up che occupational hierarchy (which is to some extent linked witch seniority in the working class)the Catholic pres is increasingly supplanced e Communis pes which  t srii when e knows ar the tendency to vote on the left varies in the same way.

Excepr for L'Humanite, the newspapers and weekly magazines only dle classes. The space mapped out by the quanciy and qualicy of reading reproduces fairly exactly the ordinary oppositions by volume and compo.
